Santee, cA 92o'lf
Re: Your Request for fnformal Assistance
Our FiIe No. I-91-07 3
Dear Mr. wilson:
This is in response to your l-etter requesting advice as a
Director of the Padre Dam Municipal Water District with respect to
the confl ict-of-interest 
provisions of the PoliticaL Reform Act
(the ttActrrl.l/ 
Since your request concerns a serj-es of decisions
coming before the district, 
we are providing you with guidelines
applicable to the decisions generally.z/
OUESTIONS
1. ltlay you participate in the Padre Dam Municipal water
District's 
consideration of revision of the $/ater conservation
ordinances ?
2. May you participate as a member of a subcommittee of the
Padre Dam Municipal water District in the consideration of a new
water rate formula necessitated by price increases by suppliers?
l/ 
covernment code sections 81ooo-91015. A11 statutory refer-
ences are to the Government code un]ess otherwise indicated- com-
rnission regulations appear at 2 california Code of Regulations
section r-8ooo, et seq. A11 references to requtations are to TitLe
2, oivision 6 of the california code of Regulations.
2/ InformaL assi,stance does not provide the requestor with the
irnrnunity provided by an opinion or forrnal written advice. (section
83114; Regulation 18329 (c) (3) . )
428 J Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 8O? o Sacramento cA 958O4-O8O7 o (916) 322'5660

Page 2
CONCLUS IONS
L 
Generally, the effects of the revj.sions to the rrater
conservation ordinance will affect the public in the same manner
as it will affect you and.your property-. However, you rray not
participate in consideration of tnL growth restriction, since the
provision will rnaterialry affect youi economic interest,s. you nay
participate in other decisions concerning revisions of the water
conservation ordinance, provided they will not have a foreseeable,
naterial financial effect on you, your property or on Net land
which is distingnrishable from the ettect on trr-e pubric aenerally.
- 
2. .- You nay_participate in the rate setting proceedings,
unless the deci-sion will have a foreseeable and-nrlterial finincial
effect on your economic interests.
FACTS
You are a ne:nber of the board of directors for the padre Dam
Municipal Water District (the 'rdistrict"). 
you are also enp:-oyea
by Newland california. ('tNewland'r) as a vice president in charge ot
a.future development in the district. 
you also own a home in-the
district.
Nehrland i-s currently planning a 3, ooo-unit residential
development in the district. 
The project is currently preparing
the specific plan for the developrnlnt. Newland tras s-ouqirt ina
obtained no pernits for the project at this point; nor fras Nevrland
requested water service fron the district. 
you inforned me in our
telephone conversation of February L3, 199t, that such an
application cannot be made until i tract map is approved, and
Newland is sti1l several steps away fron developing a tract map.
You anticipate at the earliest that the development will begin-
construction in 1993. Newland orrns no other llnd in the di;trict.
The district 
is currently considering tno actions in response
to the water shortage.
(1) The district 
is considering a series of changes to the
existing water conservation ordinance which include a growth
restriction provj,sion, as vrel1 as general conservation provisions
such as a system of odd/even watering days.
(2) The board has appointed you and one other mernber to a
subcomrnittee to develop a new rate system for custotners. The
nodification of rates was necess j.tated by a dranatic price change
by suppliers. 
The subcommittee will be setting rates similar to
the suppliersi systens which will be generally applicable
throughout the district, 
You stated a base l-eveI of water usage
will be set up, providing water at a certaj-n cost. 
Where
household usage exceeds the base Iine, the cost will jurnp to a
higher 1evel . 
Cornmercial property will be subject to the sane


Our File Noo I-9■
-073
Page 3
rates; each conmercial or governmental property is reguired to
purchase multiple units eguivalent to the units purchased by
residentiat users. For example, if a large buildj-ng uses the
equivalent of 10 households, the baseline rate will be applied to
each equivalent unit dweIling.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits any public officials 
from makingr,
participating in making, or othen'rise using their official
position to influence a governmental decision in $/hich they have a
financial interest. 
As a member of the board of directors of the
district, 
you are a public official 
under the Act. 
(Section
82o4a; Allen Advice Letter, No. A-89-708, copy enclosed.)
section 87103 specifies that an official 
has a financial
interest in a governmental decision if j.t is reasonably foresee-
able that the decision will have a naterial financial effect,
distingruishable fron the effect on the public generally, on the
official 
or a member of his or her irnrnediate farnily or on:
(b) Any real property in which the public
official, has a direct or indirect interest r.rorth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.
(c) Any source of income aggregating tvo
hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
provided to, received by, or Promised to the public
official 
wj.thin 12 months prior to the tirne vhen
the deci,sion is made.
(d) Any business entity in which the public
officiat 
is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
ernployee, or holds any position of management.
Section 87103 (b), (c) and (d).
According to Section 87103(b), your home is an econornic
interest if your interest in your horne is greater than S1'000. In
addition, you are currently enpLoyed with Newland California, and,
Newland has been a source of income to you of over $25o in the
past 12 months. Thus, Newland is an econornic interest. 
(Section
871o3(c) and (d).) 
Finally, decisions that directly affect you
financially are also treated as decisions in which you have an
economic interest. 
consequentlYr You are required to disqualify
yourself frorn district decisions where it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision lrilf have a material financial
effect on Newland or on your own incorne, assets or exPenses, or
property j.f the effects are distinguishable from the effect on the
public Aenerally.


Our Fi■e No. I-9■-073
Page 4
whether the financial conseguences of a decision are reason-
ably foreseeable at the tirne a qovernmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case. An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
Iikelihood that it hril1 occur. certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, 
it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198,
copy enclosed. )
l-. Effects on Your Propertv or on You Personallv
Any rate structure or water conservation decision that is
made by the district will have a certain econonic effect on you
personally and potentially on your property because you live in
the district 
and wiII be subject to any district 
decisions. It is
also foreseeable that in rnan! cases th6 effect will be naterial.3/
For example, negulation 18702.1(a) (4) (copy enclosed)
provides that a decision which wil-I cause the personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities 
of a Pub1ic offici,al to increase or
decrease by $250 is deemed material. 
(Regulation La702.1(a) (4);
Torrance Advice Letter, No. I-89-142, copy enclosed.) Further,
\^/here the effect on your property meets the standards in
Regulation 18702.3(c) (copy enclosed) the effect is deemed
rnaterial.
However, an official with a disqualifying financial interest
rnay still 
participate in a governrnental decision if the effect on
the officiat 
is indistinguishable from the effect on the public
generally. For the public generally exception to app1y, a
decision must affect your interests in substantially the sane
manner as it would affect a significant segrrnent of the residents
and persons doing business in the jurisdiction. 
(Regulation
L8703, copy enclosed. )
Tn 1976, in In re owen (2 FPPC ops. 77, copy enclosed), the
Cornmission considered whether the effect of a series of land use
decisions on the property interests of city councilmembers would
be distinguishable from the effect of the decisions on the public
generally. The Cornmission concluded residential homeowners in and
immediately adjacent to the core area of the city were a
significant segment of the jurisdiction and would be similarly
affected. Thus, officials 
owning residential properties could
participate,
rt 
This is especially true with respect to the rate structure
that is set up by the district. 
Other decisions such as staggered
watering schedules would not appear to have a rnaterial financial
affect on your interests.


Our Fl■e No. ■-9■―o73
Page 5
Based on the facts you have provided, it appears the
rationale in owen wou),d ipply to lour interests- ind rnost of the
decisi.ons of the water district 
concerning conservation and water
f?tes: 
However, please note, if the rate structure or any other
district 
decision will affect you or your property diff-rlntly
than it affects other owners oi residentiai piopeity i" ifr.
district, 
you are required to disgualify youiself tion
participating in the decision.
2. 
Newland
You stated that the district is considering a series of
changes to the existing water conservation ordinance. you have
specifically asked about a provision restricting growth in the
district. 
crearly a decision which affects the-a6i1ity of Newr.and
to develop the property it owns in the district would iave a
financial effect on Newland. Thus, rre would conclude that such a
decision restricting devetopnent in the district would have a
reasonably foreseeable financial effect on your econonic interest.
.Regulation tB702.I provides that a public official nay not
participate in a decision and disqual ifilation is required where
there is a nexus between the purpose for which an off-iciaI
receives incorne and a governrnentil decision. A rnexusr exists if
the official receives incorne to achieve a goal or purpose which
would be achieved, defeated, aided, or hindered by- thL
govern:rental decision. . In other words, the official nay not
acco:nplish in his_public capacity what he is paid to aciornplish in
his private capacity. (Spraque Advice Letter, No. I-88-I9-O; Chin
Advice Letter, No. A-BB-091, copies enclosed.)
You are the Vice President of Newland in charge of the
planned developrnent in Santee. Thus, you are being paid to
develop property, the developnent of which might Ue liaea or
hindered by your vote on a growth control ordinance. Thus, you
may not participate in the growth control decision.
Other water conservation issues may be separable from the
growth restriction provision. ceneral-ly, every governmental
decj-sion must be analyzed independently with respect to the
foreseeability and the materiality of a financial effect on an
official ,s economic interest. (In re O$ren, supra. ) However,
under some circunstances a series of decisions may be too inter-
related to be considered separately. (MiIIer Advice Letter, No.
A-82-119, copy enclosed.) For example, where a separate decision
could in fact alter the previous decision fron whiih you were
disqualified, the decisions would be too interrelated for you to
participate in either. 
(Nord Advice Letter, No. A-82-038, copy
enclosed. )
A.


Our Fi■e No. I-91-073
Page 6
conversely, rrhere the decisions are separable, each project
may be analyzed _ separately to determine if you have a contlict ot
interest. (Kilian Advice Letter, No. A-89-522, copy enclo-ed,)
J-f y"" have no independent conflict of interes! wi,-tir respect to
the segrmented decisions, you :nay participate.
where you can vote on some provisions and not others, the
following procedure should be used:
(1) The decislons-for which you have a disqualifying
financial interest should be segrelated fron the other d"6i=io.r..
(2) The decisions fron which you are disgualifled should be
considered first, and a final decis].on reached -by the t";;e
without your involvenent.
(3) Once a decision has been nade on the provision for vrhich
you have a.disqualifying financial interest, yoir may participate
in the deliberations on the other provisions,-so loig'.= ifro=e
decisions wil,l not result in a reopening or in any wiy affect the
decisions frora which you were disqualified. (Hufiakei eavice
Letter, No. A-86-343, copy enclosed.)
The board has appointed you and one other menber to a
subcornmittee to develop a ne!, rate system for customers. you
stated that the new rates lrourd be generally applicabre throughout
the district. 
A decision to increaie the c-ost-6f water proviied
to the resi.dents in the district would appear to have an- irnnediate
foreseeable financiar effect on Newland ii Newland was receivingr
water servj.ce or if Newland had a pending application for
additional service. Ho$/ever, you itated-thil water service is
only permitted for projects that have been approved by the city of
Santee. Newland has not yet received approvil for itl proposed
developnent. You stated that the beginninq of construclioir is
stiLl years away. Hohrever, an increise in the cost of water in
the district could foreseeably effect the ability of Newl-and to
seIl the hornes in the future. Further, the increase cost could be
lpplied directly to Newland where Newland had to purchase water
during construction. Thus, a financial inrpact on Newland is
foreseeabLe.
However, the effect on Ne$rland nust also be material to
require disqual ification. 
Whether the j_ndirect effect of an
decision is rnaterial depends on the financial size of the business
entity. Regulation L8702.2 (copy enclosed) provides guidetines
regarding when an effect on a business entity is rnateiiaL based on
the financial size of the business entity, For exarnple, if
Newland is listed on the Fortune Magazine Directory of the SOO
